title: exit code of 0 if container failed to start
body:
```
$ podman create --name=123 alpine /123
# Return 0 on a created container is expected.
$ podman wait
0
$ podman start 123
Error: unable to start container "c35e9c150e0d8b749793c9ef8d95872beb8a2be8efbcfb124cf026cb8dfaffd4": crun: executable file `123123` not found in $PATH: No such file or directory: OCI runtime attempted to invoke a command that was not found 
# Return 0 is unexpected since the container clearly exited non-0.
$ podman wait 123
0
```

Note that this behavior predates the recent exit-code changes but I found the behavior while adding new tests.  Docker properly returns/exits with 127 in the upper example.

I'll soon add a new test with a reference on this issue.

@mheon FYI

comments.body:
I have experienced the same thing with a few codepaths that succeed on create but fail on start. I think this is somewhat expected behavior (given the way our code is written) since checking some of these would mean executing the runtime code while creating the container which cannot happen.

I agree it is weird but the only way around it is to mock start the container to catch some runtime failures, right? or we would have to seriously overhaul our argument parsing.

We can, at the very least, refuse to exit cleanly if the OCI runtime did not exit cleanly when starting. To me, this smells like a failure in our parsing logic that attempts to determine if the OCI runtime failure was ENOENT - either it's not parsing that error correctly, or it's not running at all for `podman start`.

> We can, at the very least, refuse to exit cleanly if the OCI runtime did not exit cleanly when starting. To me, this smells like a failure in our parsing logic that attempts to determine if the OCI runtime failure was ENOENT - either it's not parsing that error correctly, or it's not running at all for `podman start`.

I think catching the issues on creation would be ideal. This happens a lot with resource limits too that aren't validated until crun gets a look at them at runtime
Cannot reproduce anymore.  Podman behaves exactly as Docker does.
